text: Dinah, Leah's daughter, went out to visit the young women of the land.
Shechem, son of Hamor the Hivite ruler of the land, saw her, seized her, and violated her.
His heart was drawn to Dinah, and he loved her and spoke tenderly to her.
Shechem asked his father Hamor to get him this girl as a wife.
Jacob heard his daughter had been defiled, but his sons were out with the livestock, so he said nothing until they came home.
Hamor, Shechem's father, went out to speak with Jacob.
Jacob's sons came in from the field furious and outraged, since Shechem had done an outrageous thing in Israel that should not be done.
Hamor said his son's heart longed for Dinah and asked them to let her marry him.
He proposed intermarriage between their peoples, giving and taking daughters.
He offered them the land, to settle, trade, and acquire property there.
Shechem himself told Jacob and Dinah's brothers he would give whatever they asked.
He said he would pay any bride price and gift, however great, only give him the young woman as wife.
Jacob's sons answered deceitfully, because their sister had been defiled.
They said they could not give their sister to an uncircumcised man, since that would be a disgrace.
They would only agree if every male among them was circumcised like themselves.
Then they would freely intermarry and become one people with them.
But if they refused circumcision, they would take Dinah and leave.
Their words pleased Hamor and Shechem.
Shechem, most honored in his father's house, did not delay, because he delighted in Jacob's daughter.
Hamor and Shechem went to the city gate and spoke to the men of the city.
They said these men were peaceful and should be allowed to settle, trade, and intermarry, since the land was large enough.
But the men would only agree if every male among them was circumcised as Jacob's people required.
They argued the livestock and property would eventually become theirs if they just agreed.
All the men of the city agreed with Hamor and Shechem, and every male was circumcised.
On the third day, while the men were still in pain, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers, took their swords and attacked the unsuspecting city, killing every male.
They killed Hamor and Shechem and took Dinah out of Shechem's house.
Jacob's other sons came upon the dead and plundered the city that had defiled their sister.
They took their flocks, herds, donkeys, and everything in the city and field.
They carried off all their wealth, children, and wives, and plundered everything in the houses.
Jacob told Simeon and Levi they had brought trouble on him, making him a stench to the Canaanites and Perizzites, who might now unite and destroy them, since they were few.
They answered, should he have treated our sister like a prostitute?
